immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 4-year-old boy presents to the clinic with an acute laceration on his knee after falling on the playground. Within minutes, localized physiological mechanisms are activated at the site of tissue injury to prevent the entry and systemic spread of pathogens. This initial defense strategy is best categorized under which of the following arms of immunity?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity (also known as natural or native immunity) provides immediate protection against microbial invasion, serving as the body's first line of defense before adaptive immunity develops.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A medical student is preparing a presentation comparing the functional arms of the human immune system. She defines the core concept of immunity to emphasize its evolutionary importance in host survival. Which of the following statements provides the most accurate definition of immunity?
a: The collection of cells, tissues, and molecules that mediate responses against tumors
The specific process by which B lymphocytes undergo somatic hypermutation
The body's resistance to diseases, particularly infectious diseases
The automatic destruction of self-reactive lymphocytes in primary lymphoid organs
The non-specific production of acute-phase reactants by hepatocytes
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Immunity is fundamentally defined as the state of resistance to diseases, particularly infectious diseases.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An investigator is writing a textbook chapter on basic immunology. He wants to clearly delineate the host framework responsible for preventing and eradicating infections. Which of the following best describes the structural and functional definition of the immune system?
a: An isolated group of primary lymphoid organs responsible for the destruction of foreign particles
The collection of cells, tissues, and molecules that mediate resistance to infections
A circulating network composed strictly of immunoglobulins and complement proteins
The systemic vascular structures that facilitate the marginating and rolling of red blood cells
The protective epithelial barriers of the skin and mucous membranes working independently
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The immune system is defined as the coordinated collection of cells, tissues, and molecules that mediate resistance to infections.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 28-year-old male accidentally steps on a rusted nail while working outdoors. Within an hour, neutrophils, macrophages, and alternative pathway complement proteins actively engage invading bacteria at the site of injury. Which of the following characteristics best describes this initial defense mechanism?
a: It requires clonal expansion of specialized lymphocytes over several days
It provides immediate protection against microbial invasion
It relies exclusively on the production of high-affinity IgG antibodies
It undergoes affinity maturation upon repeated exposures to the same pathogen
It is mediated primarily by CD4+ helper T cells reading MHC class II molecules
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate, natural, or native immunity acts as the first line of defense, providing immediate protection against microbial invasion.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 14-year-old girl is exposed to a classmate with varicella-zoster virus. While her innate immune system responds immediately, her body simultaneously initiates a highly specific, long-term protective response that takes days to weeks to mature. Which of the following best characterizes this secondary arm of defense?
a: It provides immediate, non-specific clearing via lysozyme secretion
It is mediated solely by pattern recognition receptors recognizing PAMPs
It develops more slowly and provides a more specialized defense against infections
It relies on preformed defensins to disrupt microbial membranes within hours
It is independent of antigen presentation or primary lymphoid organ education
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity develops more slowly than innate immunity but provides a highly specialized and potent defense against specific infections.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 30-year-old female is evaluated for an active intracellular viral infection. Her immune system utilizes two distinct arms of adaptive immunity to clear the pathogen and neutralize free viral particles. Which of the following pairs correctly matches the two types of adaptive immunity with their primary cellular mediators?
a: Cell-mediated (Dendritic cells) and Humoral (Natural Killer cells)
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity consists of two types: cell-mediated immunity (mediated by T lymphocytes) and humoral immunity (mediated by B lymphocytes).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A biopsy of an acutely inflamed subcutaneous tissue sample is examined under a microscope. The tissue is flooded with cell types belonging purely to the cellular component of the innate immune system. Which of the following groups of cells represents cellular components of innate immunity?
a: B lymphocytes, T lymphocytes, and Plasma cells
Helper T cells, Cytotoxic T cells, and Memory B cells
Plasma cells, Follicular dendritic cells, and Lymphoblasts
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The cellular components of the innate immune system include phagocytes and antigen-presenting cells such as neutrophils, macrophages, and dendritic cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An experimental model evaluates the early chemical and cellular defenses of a host animal within the first 6 hours of a bacterial inoculation. Which of the following sets of features and molecules is exclusively associated with this innate immune response?
a: Highly specific clonal selection, V(D)J recombination, and IgG production
Non-specific, immediately active, lysozyme, complements, defensins, and toll-like receptors
T-cell receptor activation, somatic hypermutation, and isotype switching
MHC Class II restriction, memory cell generation, and IgA secretion
B-cell receptor affinity maturation and plasma cell differentiation
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity features non-specific, immediate responses mediated by chemical and cellular barriers such as lysozyme, complement proteins, defensins, and toll-like receptors (TLRs).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 5-year-old child receives a booster vaccine. Over the next two weeks, his immune system mounts a highly specific response tailored precisely to the vaccine antigens, characterized by lymphocyte proliferation and antibody secretion. Which of the following combinations of mediators is responsible for these adaptive features?
a: Complement proteins, Toll-like receptors, and Defensins
T and B lymphocytes, high specificity, and Immunoglobulins secreted by B cells
Dendritic cells, ssRNA, and Pathogen-Associated Molecular Patterns
Natural Killer cells, Interferon alpha, and Interferon beta
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity is characterized by T and B lymphocytes, exquisite antigenic specificity, and the production of highly specialized immunoglobulins (antibodies) by B-cell-derived plasma cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An in vitro study examines how macrophages recognize invading bacterial pathogens. The cells successfully identify conserved, invariant microbial structures using specialized surface receptors. Which of the following targets does the innate immune system recognize to initiate this defensive response?
a: Highly variable idiotypes via T-cell receptors
Pathogen-Associated Molecular Patterns (PAMPs) via Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRRs)
MHC Class I molecules displaying mutated self-peptides
Hypervariable loops located on secretory IgA dimeric complexes
Fab fragments derived from circulating host immunoglobulins
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The innate immune system recognizes Pathogen-Associated Molecular Patterns (PAMPs) using hardwired host Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRRs).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A laboratory technician isolates mature lymphocytes from a healthy donor and exposes them to an antigen. The cells bind the antigen with extreme specificity using highly variable surface membrane receptors. Which of the following pairs of molecules represents the primary antigen recognition receptors of adaptive immunity?
a: Toll-like receptors (TLRs) and Pathogen-Associated Molecular Patterns (PAMPs)
B cell Receptors (BCR) and T Cell Receptors (TCR)
Complement component C3b and Fc-gamma receptors
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The adaptive immune system achieves high target specificity through the expression of B-cell receptors (BCRs) and T-cell receptors (TCRs).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A molecular biology laboratory isolates a family of germline-encoded host receptors expressed on macrophages, dendritic cells, and barrier epithelia. These receptors function specifically to sense the presence of microbial invaders by binding to conserved pathogen structures. Which of the following general terms best describes this class of innate immune receptors?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRRs) are germline-encoded host receptors that recognize conserved microbial structures known as PAMPs.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A biopsy of the skin from a patient with a localized Gram-positive bacterial infection shows marked activation of resident dendritic cells. Molecular analysis demonstrates that this activation was initiated when host cell surface receptors bound to bacterial cell wall peptidoglycans. Which of the following Pattern Recognition Receptors is specifically responsible for sensing this Gram-positive microbial component?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Toll-like Receptor 2 (TLR-2) recognizes peptidoglycan, a major cell wall component of Gram-positive bacteria.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 42-year-old male develops severe endotoxemia and septic shock due to a fulminant Gram-negative bacterial infection. The intense systemic inflammatory cascade is triggered when bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) binds to a specific host toll-like receptor. Which of the following receptors is responsible for binding LPS?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Toll-like Receptor 4 (TLR-4) specifically recognizes and binds lipopolysaccharide (LPS), an endotoxin found in the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A researcher constructs a mutant strain of motile bacteria that lacks functional flagella. When these mutant bacteria are introduced to a culture of wild-type macrophages, the host cells exhibit a significantly blunted innate immune response compared to when they are exposed to the motile wild-type strain. Which of the following Toll-like receptors was failured to activate by this mutant bacterial strain?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Toll-like Receptor 5 (TLR-5) specifically recognizes flagellin, the principal protein component of bacterial flagella.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An experimental model evaluates host endosomal responses to an infection by an enveloped single-stranded RNA (ssRNA) virus. Within the endosomes of plasmacytoid dendritic cells, specific pattern recognition receptors bind the viral nucleic acids, triggering an immediate antiviral signaling pathway. Which of the following Toll-like receptors mediates this response?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Toll-like Receptors 7 and 8 (TLR-7/8) are located within endosomes and specifically recognize single-stranded RNA (ssRNA), a common viral genetic material.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 6-year-old boy contracts a viral infection characterized by intracellular replication that produces double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) intermediates. Endosomal receptors within his host cells detect these dsRNA structures to activate Type I Interferon production. Which of the following Toll-like receptors is responsible for detecting dsRNA?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Toll-like Receptor 3 (TLR-3) is specialized in recognizing double-stranded RNA (dsRNA), which serves as a marker for many viral infections.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Ligand binding to Toll-like receptors activates an intracellular signaling cascade that culminates in the translocation of a key transcription factor from the cytoplasm into the nucleus. This transcription factor then drives the expression of numerous pro-inflammatory genes. Which of the following molecules matches this description?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NF-kB (Nuclear Factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells) is a major downstream transcription factor activated by PRR signaling.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A molecular biology study investigates the central controllers of the host defense response. Researchers identify an ancient, highly conserved transcription factor complex that acts as a master switch, regulating the transcription of genes necessary for both immediate non-specific defenses and long-term adaptive immunity. Which of the following factors is being studied?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NF-kB is a major transcription factor that regulates a vast array of genes driving the immune response.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An experimental knockout mouse model completely lacks functional NF-kB. When exposed to pathogens, these mice demonstrate a profound failure to produce inflammatory cytokines and a simultaneous defect in antigen-specific lymphocyte proliferation. Which of the following statements best explains these concurrent deficits?
a: NF-kB is responsible exclusively for the somatic hypermutation of immunoglobulins
NF-kB is a major transcription factor responsible for both innate (cytokine expression) and adaptive immune responses (B and T cell pathways)
NF-kB regulates the expression of selectins but has no downstream nuclear effects
NF-kB acts only within primary lymphoid organs to delete self-reactive clones
NF-kB serves as a membrane receptor for bacterial flagellin proteins
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NF-kB is responsible for both innate immune responses (such as the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines) and adaptive immune responses (modulating B-cell and T-cell activation and survival).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 19-year-old male is studying host defense mechanisms. He wants to catalog the physical and chemical barriers that prevent microorganisms from crossing epithelial surfaces and colonizing tissues. Which of the following groups represents components of these baseline epithelial/surface defenses?
a: T-cell receptors, B-cell receptors, and IgG
Skin, lysozyme in tears, respiratory cilia, surface phagocytes, defensins, and normal microbiota
Lymph nodes, spleen, paracortex, and marginal zones
Bone marrow, thymus, and secondary follicles
Interferon alpha, interferon beta, and inflammasomes
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Baseline physical and chemical barriers include the skin, lysozyme (found in tears), respiratory cilia, surface phagocytes, defensins, and the protective normal microbiota.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 31-year-old male is infected with a respiratory virus. His body utilizes several innate defense mechanisms to limit viral replication and microenvironment bacterial growth without relying on prior antigen exposure. Which of the following combinations represents innate factors that limit the growth of microbes in the body?
a: T-cell receptors, B-cell receptors, IgA, IgG, and IgM
NK cells, neutrophils, macrophages, IFN alpha/beta, iron-binding proteins (transferrin/lactoferrin/ferritin), fever, and the inflammasome
Bone marrow, thymus, lymph nodes, and mucosal-associated lymphoid tissue
CD18 integrins, ICAM-1, VCAM-1, and PECAM-1
Sialyl-Lewis X, E-selectin, P-selectin, and CD31
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate factors that limit microbial growth include NK cells (killing virus-infected cells), neutrophils and macrophages (phagocytosis), IFN alpha/beta (eliminating viruses), iron-binding proteins (transferrin/lactoferrin/ferritin), fever, and the inflammasome complex.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A dendritic cell in the skin captures an exogenous protein antigen from an invading bacterium, internalizes it, processes it into small peptide fragments, and migrates to a local lymph node. There, it displays the peptide on its cell surface bound to an MHC molecule to activate naive helper T cells. Which of the following terms best describes the role of this dendritic cell?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: An Antigen Presenting Cell (APC) captures foreign invaders (antigens), processes them, and shows a piece on its surface to the adaptive immune system (specifically T cells).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An immunologist studies the cellular interactions required to initiate a T-cell-mediated immune response. He notes that only a select group of immune cells express Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) Class II molecules constitutively and possess the co-stimulatory machinery necessary to activate naive T lymphocytes. Which of the following sets of cells are classified as professional APCs?
a: Dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells
Natural Killer cells, mast cells, and plasma cells
Helper T cells, cytotoxic T cells, and regulatory T cells
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The professional antigen-presenting cells (APCs) are dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A medical student is constructing a diagram to illustrate the life cycle of human lymphocytes. She groups the organs of the lymphoid system based on whether they serve as sites of initial cell production and maturation or sites of subsequent antigen encounter. Which of the following organs comprise the primary lymphoid organs?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The primary (central) lymphoid organs are the bone marrow and the thymus, where lymphocytes are generated and mature.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 12-year-old child develops an infection in his right foot. Pathogens and floating antigens travel via afferent lymphatic vessels into the popliteal and inguinal lymph nodes, triggering a localized immune response. Which of the following groups contains only secondary lymphoid organs where these adaptive immune responses are initiated?
a: Lymph nodes, spleen, and MALT (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue)
Yellow bone marrow and umbilical cord blood
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The secondary (peripheral) lymphoid organs include the lymph nodes, spleen, and mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An experimental drug selectively destroys the cellular niches within the human bone marrow. While mature T cells are unaffected, the production of naive B lymphocytes drops to zero. Which of the following processes has been disrupted by this drug?
a: The maturation of T cells within the thymic cortex
The development and maturation of B cells
The filtration of blood-borne antigens by splenic macrophages
The activation of memory T cells within the lymph node paracortex
The somatic hypermutation of IgA antibodies in mucosal linings
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The bone marrow is the primary lymphoid organ responsible for the development and maturation of B cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A newborn infant is found to have a congenital defect causing severe thymic hypoplasia. Laboratory analysis reveals a profound deficiency of circulating, mature T lymphocytes, whereas mature B lymphocyte counts are normal. Which of the following processes is dependent on the functional tissue of the thymus?
a: The primary development and selection of T cells
The differentiation of B cells into plasma cells
The production of acute-phase reactants like CRP
The alternative pathway activation of complement proteins
The rolling of neutrophils along endothelial selectins
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The thymus is the primary lymphoid organ dedicated to the development, differentiation, and selection of T cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 23-year-old male is exposed to a novel bacterial pathogen. Antigens from the bacterium drain into his peripheral lymphoid tissues, where they encounter naive B and T lymphocytes. Which of the following best describes the primary purpose of secondary lymphoid organs, such as lymph nodes and the spleen?
a: They serve as the sites of antigen-independent lymphocyte production
They are the exclusive sites of T and B cell activation by antigen
They manufacture iron-binding proteins like transferrin and lactoferrin
They host the initial steps of the neutrophil respiratory burst
They produce preformed lysozyme and defensin molecules
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Secondary lymphoid organs are specialized anatomical sites designed to serve as the presentation grounds where T and B cells are activated by foreign antigens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 35-year-old female develops a localized skin infection on her right arm following a superficial scrape. Bacterial antigens successfully cross the epithelial barrier and enter the local interstitial fluid. Which of the following organs is anatomically designed to filter this fluid and initiate immune responses against tissue-borne antigens?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Lymph nodes are organized, encapsulated structures designed to initiate adaptive immune responses against tissue-borne antigens brought via lymphatic drainage.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An immunologist uses immunofluorescence to trace the migration of naive B cells within a lymph node following an active infection. She observes these cells proliferating and forming germinal centers within the outermost structural layer of the node. Which of the following specific regions is the primary site of B cell localization and activation?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The cortex of the lymph node is the anatomical zone containing lymphoid follicles, serving as the site of B-cell localization, activation, and proliferation.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A viral infection stimulates a robust cell-mediated immune response within a regional lymph node. Histological examination reveals prominent hypertrophy of the anatomical zone located directly between the outer cortex and the inner medulla. Which of the following regions is the primary site of T cell localization and activation within the lymph node?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The paracortex is the T-cell rich zone of the lymph node where T cells reside, encounter antigens presented by dendritic cells, and undergo activation.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 22-year-old male develops an intravascular bacterial infection following an contaminated intravenous injection. The bacteria replicate directly within his bloodstream. Which of the following organs is specifically designed to filter the blood and initiate adaptive immune responses against blood-borne antigens?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The spleen is the secondary lymphoid organ responsible for filtering systemic circulation and initiating immune responses against blood-borne antigens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 19-year-old male with sickle cell disease presents with a high fever, chills, and altered mental status. Blood cultures grow Streptococcus pneumoniae. His severe infection is primarily due to functional asplenia caused by recurrent splenic infarctions. Which of the following specific anatomical components of the spleen is compromised, resulting in this vulnerability?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Splenic dysfunction (seen post-splenectomy or in sickle cell auto-splenectomy) disrupts the splenic marginal zone, which is critical for capturing encapsulated organisms.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A pathology resident reviews a histology slide of a healthy human spleen. He identifies a specialized transition zone that directly surrounds the lymphoid follicles, separating the white pulp from the red pulp. Which of the following terms correctly identifies this structural region?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The splenic marginal zone is the distinct, specialized boundary area that surrounds the lymphoid follicles.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 25-year-old male undergoes an emergency splenectomy following a severe motor vehicle accident. Over the next several years, he is at an increased risk for life-threatening sepsis. He must receive targeted immunizations to protect against which of the following classes of organisms?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The loss of the spleen and its marginal zone results in a selective susceptibility to encapsulated organisms, notably Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A study characterizes the cellular mechanisms underlying the immunodeficiency state that follows a total splenectomy. Researchers determine that the loss of specialized phagocytic cells and specialized antibody-producing cells within the marginal zone directly accounts for the blunted systemic response. Which of the following pairs of cellular changes correctly matches this pathology?
a: Loss of neutrophils (reduction in HOCl) and loss of T cells (reduction in IgA)
Loss of marginal zone macrophages (reduction in phagocytosis) and loss of marginal zone B cells (reduction in IgM)
Loss of dendritic cells (reduction in TLRs) and loss of plasma cells (reduction in IgE)
Loss of NK cells (reduction in viral killing) and loss of eosinophils (reduction in histamine)
Loss of endothelial cells (reduction in ICAM-1) and loss of mast cells (reduction in PGE2)
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Splenectomy results in the loss of marginal zone macrophages, causing a reduction in phagocytosis, and the loss of marginal zone B cells, causing a profound reduction in circulating IgM.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 3-year-old child undergoes a total splenectomy due to severe hereditary spherocytosis. Following the surgery, the pediatrician updates the child's immunization record and outlines a strict prophylactic management plan to prevent overwhelming post-splenectomy sepsis. Which of the following interventions represents standard clinical protocol for this young patient?
a: Continuous antibiotic prophylaxis, typically oral amoxicillin, at least to age 5
Avoidance of all encapsulated vaccines until adulthood
Isolation from normal microbiota exposures in the community
Daily administration of high-dose corticosteroid anti-inflammatory drugs
Intravenous infusions of preformed defensins and lysozyme every month
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Patients undergoing splenectomy require appropriate vaccinations and continuous antibiotic prophylaxis (such as oral amoxicillin), particularly children up to at least age 5.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 45-year-old female presents for a routine check-up six months after undergoing a splenectomy. A peripheral blood smear is performed to evaluate her red blood cell morphology. Which of the following microscopic findings is a classic laboratory sign of an absent or non-functional spleen?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Howell-Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants within erythrocytes normally removed by the spleen) are a classic peripheral blood finding post-splenectomy.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A hematology researcher traces the differentiation pathways of pluripotent hematopoietic stem cells within the bone marrow. She observes a lineage that gives rise to platelets, erythrocytes, eosinophils, basophils, neutrophils, and monocytes. Which of the following lineages is responsible for generating these cell types?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Cells of the myeloid lineage differentiate into platelets, erythrocytes, eosinophils, basophils, neutrophils, and monocytes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An investigator studies the cellular branches of hematopoiesis. He focuses on a developmental pathway that originates in the primary lymphoid tissues and selectively produces adaptive lymphocytes along with a subset of innate cytotoxic cells. Which of the following lineages differentiates into T lymphocytes, B lymphocytes, NK cells, and some dendritic cells?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Cells of the lymphoid lineage differentiate into T and B lymphocytes, Natural Killer (NK) cells, and certain populations of dendritic cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A laboratory assay evaluates the ability of human neutrophils to bind and ingest opsonized pathogens. To successfully bind bacteria coated with Immunoglobulin G (IgG), the neutrophil must express specific surface receptors that recognize the constant structural tail of the antibody. Which of the following receptors is expressed on the surface of neutrophils to bind IgG?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Neutrophils express surface Fc receptors (FcRs) that specifically bind to the Fc portion of IgG antibodies coating a pathogen.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 54-year-old male undergoing intensive chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukemia develops severe neutropenia, with an absolute neutrophil count of 150 cells/µL. Over the next week, he is at an increased risk for developing life-threatening opportunistic infections. Which of the following groups of pathogens represents the most common threats to a neutropenic patient?
a: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae
Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, and Aspergillus
Varicella-zoster virus, Human immunodeficiency virus, and Cytomegalovirus
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Salmonella enterica, and Listeria
Giardia lamblia, Entamoeba histolytica, and Cryptosporidium
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Patients with severe neutropenia are highly vulnerable to specific opportunistic bacterial and fungal pathogens, most notably Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, and Aspergillus.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An immunologist maps the expression of Fc receptors (FcRs) across various white blood cell populations. She notes that these specialized receptors allow innate immune cells to link with the adaptive immune system by binding antibody-coated targets. Which of the following groups of cells express functional Fc receptors on their surface?
a: T lymphocytes, B lymphocytes, and Plasma cells
Neutrophils, Macrophages, NK cells, and Eosinophils
Thymic epithelial cells and Follicular dendritic cells
Helper T cells, Cytotoxic T cells, and Regulatory T cells
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Fc receptors (FcRs) are expressed on the surface of several innate immune cells, including neutrophils, macrophages, Natural Killer (NK) cells, and eosinophils.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A basic science experiment evaluates the exact sequence of events required for efficient macrophage-mediated phagocytosis of a target pathogen. For the macrophage to successfully engage its surface Fc receptors and engulf the invader, which of the following steps must occur first?
a: The pathogen must secrete NF-kB into the surrounding extracellular matrix
IgG must first coat the surface of the pathogen and then bind to the Fc receptor
The macrophage must secrete lysozyme to alter the antibody's idiotype
Complement protein C3b must directly cleave the Fc receptor's binding domain
The pathogen must downregulate its Pathogen-Associated Molecular Patterns
immunity_and_immune_system.l: During opsonization, IgG antibodies first coat the surface of the invading pathogen and subsequently bind to the host cell's surface Fc receptors.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: A 10-year-old boy is infected with an intestinal helminth. His immune system deploys a specialized granulocytic cell type that binds to the antibody-coated parasite and releases toxic major basic protein from its granules. Which of the following cells possesses Fc receptors configured to bind IgG and IgE to coordinate this defense?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Eosinophils express surface Fc receptors capable of binding to both IgG and IgE, which is essential for their specialized defense against parasitic infections and participation in allergic responses.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 24-year-old male sustains a deep laceration on his thigh while working on a construction site. Within hours, the area becomes erythematous, warm, and swollen. This immediate localized response represents the body's primary reaction to tissue injury. Which of the following best describes this physiological process?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Inflammation is the body's first response to injury, infection, or tissue damage, serving to deliver defensive materials to the site of insult.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 45-year-old female presents with a 1-day history of acute localized pain and swelling in her right forearm following an insect bite. A biopsy of the subcutaneous tissue is performed. Which of the following cell types is expected to predominate in this tissue under conditions of acute inflammation?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Neutrophils are the primary cellular mediators that predominate during acute inflammation, typically arriving within the first 6 to 24 hours.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 52-year-old male undergoes a biopsy of a persistent, non-healing skin lesion that has been present for four months. Histopathological evaluation reveals a cellular infiltrate consisting primarily of mononuclear cells. Which of the following sets of cells is expected to predominate in this chronic inflammatory state?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chronic inflammation is characterized by a prolonged duration in which mononuclear cells, specifically macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells, predominate.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 7-year-old boy presents with a painful, fluctuant fluctuant swelling on his scalp. Incision and drainage are performed, yielding a thick, creamy, neutrophil-rich purulent exudate. Culture grows Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following terms best describes infections caused by this class of organisms?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Pyogenic infections, typically caused by organisms like Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and Neisseria, are characterized by neutrophil-rich pus formation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 34-year-old female presents with chronic cough and weight loss. A chest X-ray reveals hilar lymphadenopathy, and a biopsy of a lymph node shows non-caseating granulomas. Histopathological evaluation shows an extensive accumulation of transformed mononuclear cells. Which of the following cell types forms the cellular core of this granulomatous inflammation?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Granulomatous infections and conditions (such as tuberculosis, fungal infections, and sarcoidosis) feature a distinct macrophage predominance, often organizing into epithelioid histiocytes.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An experimental model of acute inflammation is used to study the sequential steps of leukocyte migration from the vascular lumen into the interstitial space. Which of the following represents the correct order of leukocyte recruitment steps?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The correct sequence of leukocyte recruitment during inflammation is: Margination → Rolling → Adhesion → Transmigration (diapedesis) → Chemotaxis.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: During an investigation into leukocyte endothelial interactions, researchers observe leukocytes moving slowly along the venular wall. This initial 'rolling' phase is dependent on transient, low-affinity interactions. Which of the following leukocyte surface molecules binds to endothelial E-selectin and P-selectin to mediate this step?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Leukocyte rolling is mediated by the binding of Sialyl-Lewis X on leukocytes to E-selectin and P-selectin expressed on the activated endothelium.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A tissue biopsy from a site of acute inflammation shows leukocytes firmly bound to the endothelial surface, resisting the shear stress of blood flow. This stable adhesion step requires high-affinity binding. Which of the following endothelial adhesion molecules binds to leukocyte integrins containing CD18 to achieve this firm adhesion?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Firm adhesion occurs when leukocyte integrins (such as CD18 complexes) bind to immunoglobulin superfamily ligands on the endothelium, specifically ICAM-1 and VCAM-1.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An investigator treats an endothelial cell monolayer with a specific neutralizing antibody, which prevents leukocytes from successfully squeezing through the intercellular junctions between adjacent endothelial cells. This intervention has targeted the step of diapedesis. Which of the following molecules is primarily responsible for mediating this transmigration step?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Transmigration (diapedesis) of leukocytes across the endothelial wall is mediated by PECAM-1 (CD31), which is expressed on both leukocytes and endothelial cell junctions.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Following exit from the postcapillary venules, neutrophils migrate through the extracellular matrix along a chemical gradient toward the focus of tissue injury. Which of the following terms best defines this directed migration process?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chemotaxis is defined as the directed migration of leukocytes through tissue toward a chemical gradient composed of inflammatory mediators or bacterial products.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A biopsy specimen demonstrates dense neutrophil infiltration within an area of ischemic tissue necrosis. Which of the following sets of endogenous and exogenous molecules serves as the major chemotactic factors driving this targeted recruitment?
a: IL-8, C5a, LTB4, and bacterial N-formyl peptides
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The major chemotactic factors for neutrophils include Interleukin-8 (IL-8), complement component C5a, Leukotriene B4 (LTB4), and exogenous bacterial N-formyl peptides.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Within minutes of exposure to an allergen, a patient develops rapid tissue edema, localized warmth, and widespread erythema. These immediate changes are caused by the release of preformed mediators from mast cells that induce vasodilation and an abrupt increase in vascular permeability. Which of the following mediators is primarily responsible for this effect?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Histamine is a vasoactive amine that induces prominent arteriolar vasodilation and increased vascular permeability in venules during the immediate phases of inflammation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 19-year-old college student presents to the student health clinic with a severe acute upper respiratory tract infection. He reports a generalized painful sensation throughout his body and has a documented oral temperature of 38.9°C (102°F). Which of the following arachidonic acid metabolites directly modulates the hypothalamic thermostat to induce fever and cause localized pain?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) is the key eicosanoid responsible for mediating pain, fever induction in the hypothalamus, and vasodilation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Researchers studying vascular homeostasis analyze an arachidonic acid derivative produced primarily by intact vascular endothelial cells. This compound prevents intravascular clot formation by robustly inhibiting platelet aggregation and promoting profound vasodilation. Which of the following mediators matches this description?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Prostacyclin (PGI2) is synthesized by endothelial cells and acts to cause vasodilation and strongly inhibit platelet aggregation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Following an acute vascular injury, platelets adhere to exposed subendothelial collagen and release a potent eicosanoid mediator. This mediator acts local to the injury to promote immediate vasoconstriction and accelerate primary hemostasis by driving platelet aggregation. Which of the following mediators is responsible for these actions?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Thromboxane A2 (TXA2) is produced by platelets and serves to promote vasoconstriction and stimulate platelet aggregation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An experimental drug blocks the production of a specific lipoxygenase pathway metabolite, resulting in a dramatic reduction in the recruitment of neutrophils to sites of sterile tissue injury. Which of the following arachidonic acid derivatives was most likely inhibited by this drug?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Leukotriene B4 (LTB4) is a highly potent lipoxygenase product that directly stimulates neutrophil chemotaxis and activation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 6-year-old child with severe asthma accidentally ingests a known trigger, resulting in acute smooth muscle contraction within her airways, profound bronchoconstriction, and prominent peribronchial edema. Which of the following groups of leukotrienes mediates these severe bronchospastic effects and increases microvascular permeability?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, and LTE4) cause intense smooth muscle contraction, leading to bronchoconstriction and markedly increased vascular permeability.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 64-year-old male is admitted to the intensive care unit with bacterial septic shock. His blood pressure is critically low, and he has an elevated core temperature. The systemic inflammatory response, including endothelial activation and fever expression, is driven by macrophages releasing which of the following pairs of cytokines?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF-alpha) and Interleukin-1 (IL-1) are principal cytokines secreted by macrophages that coordinate fever induction and local endothelial activation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 48-year-old male is evaluated for a severe intra-abdominal abscess. Laboratory studies reveal a markedly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and high serum levels of C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following cytokines is primarily responsible for signaling hepatocytes to synthesize and secrete these acute-phase reactants?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is the major cytokine responsible for stimulating hepatocytes to produce acute-phase reactants, including CRP and fibrinogen.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 22-year-old female is evaluated for a new-onset febrile illness accompanied by joint pain. Serologic testing demonstrates high titers of antigen-specific antibodies configured as a large pentameric structure. This finding confirms an acute, primary immune response. Which of the following immunoglobulins is the first antibody produced during an acute infection?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Immunoglobulin M (IgM) is the first antibody isotype synthesized during a primary immune response, indicating acute or recent infection.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 29-year-old pregnant woman is evaluated at her routine obstetric appointment. She expresses relief that her baby will have passive immunity against several childhood diseases at birth. Which of the following immunoglobulins mediates this secondary immune response, marks past infection, and has the unique capacity to cross the human placenta?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Immunoglobulin G (IgG) dominates the secondary immune response, indicates chronic or past infection, and is the only antibody class capable of crossing the placenta.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A researcher isolates immunoglobulin molecules from a sample of healthy breast milk and saliva. The antibodies are found to exist primarily as dimeric structures joined by a J chain and a secretory component. Which of the following immunoglobulins is responsible for providing this mucosal immunity?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Immunoglobulin A (IgA) provides critical mucosal immunity and is the predominant antibody found in secretions such as saliva, tears, and breast milk.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 12-year-old boy experiences severe wheezing, pruritus, and urticaria immediately after being stung by a wasp. Laboratory evaluations also note an elevated peripheral blood eosinophil count. Which of the following immunoglobulins binds to high-affinity receptors on mast cells and basophils to mediate allergic reactions and defend against parasitic infections?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Immunoglobulin E (IgE) is involved in mediating Type I hypersensitivity (allergic) reactions and defending the host against parasitic helminth infections.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An immunologist characterizes a class of immunoglobulins found almost exclusively anchored within the plasma membranes of naive, unactivated mature B lymphocytes, acting alongside monomeric IgM. Which of the following immunoglobulins serves primarily as this low-abundance surface B-cell receptor?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Immunoglobulin D (IgD) is expressed primarily on the surface of naive B cells, where it functions as a antigen-binding B-cell receptor.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A molecular biology study evaluates the efficiency of neutrophil phagocytosis. When bacteria are incubated alone with neutrophils, ingestion proceeds slowly. However, adding specific serum proteins results in the coating of the microbial surfaces, accelerating phagocytic uptake. Which of the following groups of molecules acts as the principal factors driving this opsonization process?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Opsonization involves the coating of microbes by specific molecules, most notably IgG, complement fragment C3b, and C-reactive protein (CRP), to enhance recognition and phagocytosis.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: During an in vitro experiment, neutrophils are exposed to opsonized bacteria. Almost immediately, a rapid increase in oxygen consumption is measured within the leukocyte population. This metabolic shift is directed toward producing microbicial reactive oxygen species. Which of the following terms best denotes this protective physiological phenomenon?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The respiratory burst (or oxidative burst) refers to the rapid metabolic upregulation of oxygen consumption by phagocytes to generate reactive oxygen species capable of destroying internalized pathogens.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An investigator studies the enzymatic sequence within the phagolysosomes of activated neutrophils. The initial step of the respiratory burst requires the rapid conversion of molecular oxygen into superoxide radicals. Which of the following enzymes catalyzes this specific opening step?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: NADPH oxidase is the enzyme complex responsible for initiating the respiratory burst by converting molecular oxygen (O2) into superoxide anions (O2-).

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Within a neutrophil phagolysosome, superoxide radicals are rapidly generated during the respiratory burst. To progress down the bactericidal pathway, an enzyme must convert these potentially dangerous superoxide anions into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Which of the following enzymes catalyzes this specific step?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Superoxide dismutase (SOD) converts the highly reactive superoxide radical (O2-) into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2).

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A laboratory study analyzes the contents of neutrophil azurophilic granules. An enzyme utilizes hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and chloride ions as substrates to produce hypochlorous acid (HOCl). Which of the following enzymes is responsible for generating this potent bactericidal compound?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Myeloperoxidase (MPO) is a granule enzyme that converts hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the presence of halides into hypochlorous acid (HOCl).

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Phagocytosed bacteria are exposed to an array of toxic oxygen-derived intermediates inside neutrophil phagolysosomes. Which of the following reactive chemicals represents the most potent neutrophil bactericidal agent generated during the normal respiratory burst?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Hypochlorous acid (HOCl), chemically identical to active bleach, is the most potent and destructive bactericidal agent manufactured by neutrophils.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 2-year-old boy is evaluated for recurrent, deep-seated bacterial skin and mucosal infections. Flow cytometry demonstrates a complete absence of the CD18 cell surface marker on his leukocytes. Which of the following conditions is characteristically caused by this molecular deficiency?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD) Type I is caused by an autosomal recessive defect in the CD18 molecule, which is the beta-2 integrin subunit.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An infant with Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency Type I is evaluated. Due to a genetically defective CD18 integrin subunit, his leukocytes are unable to interact properly with ligands on the vascular wall. Which of the following steps of leukocyte recruitment is directly disrupted by this specific mutation?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: LAD Type I features a profound impairment in firm adhesion and subsequent transmigration (diapedesis) because neutrophils lack functional integrins.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: The parents of a 6-week-old infant bring him to the pediatrician because his umbilical cord stump has still not detached. On examination, the area is clean with no significant erythema, but a complete blood count reveals a marked peripheral leukocytosis. This classic presentation is associated with which of the following immunodeficiencies?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: A classic clinical hallmark of Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD) Type I is the delayed separation of the umbilical cord stump.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 14-month-old child presents with a severe perianal ulcer. Culture isolates Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Despite the clinical depth and severity of the infection, physical examination reveals an absence of fluctuance or localized purulence, and biopsy confirms an absence of neutrophils within the necrotic tissue. Which of the following primary disorders explains this clinical presentation?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: LAD Type I presents with recurrent bacterial infections characterized by an absence of pus formation at infection sites, despite high white blood cell counts in circulation.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A patient is diagnosed with Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD) Type II. Genetic testing confirms a loss-of-function mutation affecting a fucose transporter, leading to a complete structural absence of Sialyl-Lewis X on the leukocyte surface. Which of the following conditions matches this specific molecular pathology?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD) Type II is caused by a defect in fucosylation, resulting in an absence of the crucial Sialyl-Lewis X ligand.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 3-year-old child with Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency Type II is evaluated. Because her neutrophils lack the functional carbohydrate ligand Sialyl-Lewis X, they cannot interact with selectins expressed on activated endothelial cells. Which of the following leukocyte recruitment stages is primarily affected?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: LAD Type II leads to defective rolling of leukocytes along the endothelium because selectins require Sialyl-Lewis X to mediate initial low-affinity interactions.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 4-year-old girl is evaluated for recurrent infections. Genetic mapping reveals an autosomal recessive mutation in the LYST gene, which regulates intracellular vesicle dynamics. Which of the following primary clinical diagnoses matches this genetic abnormality?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chediak-Higashi syndrome is caused by a loss-of-function mutation in the lysosomal trafficking regulator (LYST) gene.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Neutrophils isolated from a patient with Chediak-Higashi syndrome are analyzed under electron microscopy. Phagocytosis occurs normally, and microbes are successfully internalized into phagosomes. However, the cells are unable to successfully fuse these compartments with azurophilic granules. Which of the following processes is defective in this patient?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chediak-Higashi syndrome features a cellular defect in phagolysosome formation due to abnormal microtubule-dependent trafficking and lysosomal fusion.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 5-year-old boy with Chediak-Higashi syndrome presents with multiple painful cutaneous abscesses. Over his lifetime, he has experienced recurrent deep tissue skin infections requiring hospitalization. Which of the following specific microbial pathogens is most commonly isolated from infections in these patients?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Patients with Chediak-Higashi syndrome suffer from recurrent, severe pyogenic infections, most frequently caused by Staphylococcus aureus.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 6-year-old child is brought to the clinic due to recurrent skin infections. On physical examination, the physician notes light silver-tinted hair, pale skin, and asymmetric nystagmus. A peripheral blood smear demonstrates neutrophils containing massive, irregular azurophilic inclusions. Which of the following disorders is consistent with this constellation of findings?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chediak-Higashi syndrome is characterized by partial oculocutaneous albinism, progressive peripheral neuropathy, recurrent infections, and giant granules inside neutrophils.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 9-year-old boy presents with recurrent lymphadenitis and deep hepatic abscesses. Genetic testing identifies an X-linked defect in a subunit of the enzyme NADPH oxidase. Which of the following immunodeficiencies is characterized by this specific enzymatic failure?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD) is an immunodeficiency disorder caused by a deficiency in the multi-subunit enzyme NADPH oxidase.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An in vitro analysis of neutrophils from a patient with Chronic Granulomatous Disease is conducted. Phagocytosis proceeds normally, but the cellular respiratory burst is absent. Which of the following downstream steps in the oxidative killing pathway is directly blocked by this patient's primary defect?
a: Inability to convert H2O2 into HOCl
Defective leukocyte rolling along endothelial selectins
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Because of the missing NADPH oxidase activity, neutrophils from patients with CGD suffer from an inability to generate superoxide radicals (O2-) from molecular oxygen.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 7-year-old boy with Chronic Granulomatous Disease presents with a severe lung infection. He has a history of recurrent infections with specific microbes that produce an enzyme capable of destroying the host cell's endogenous hydrogen peroxide. Which of the following classes of microbes poses the greatest threat to this patient?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: CGD patients are highly susceptible to recurrent infections with catalase-positive organisms, which neutralize their own H2O2, leaving the host cells without any source of reactive oxygen.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 10-year-old boy with X-linked Chronic Granulomatous Disease is hospitalized with osteomyelitis. Cultures isolate a specific opportunistic pathogen. Which of the following groups contains only organisms that are characteristically catalase-positive and frequently isolated in patients with CGD?
a: S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, and H. influenzae
S. aureus, Aspergillus, Serratia, Nocardia, and Burkholderia cepacia
C. perfringens, B. fragilis, and F. nucleatum
C. albicans, C. neoformans, and H. capsulatum
M. tuberculosis, M. leprae, and M. avium
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The major catalase-positive pathogens causing recurrent infections in CGD include Staphylococcus aureus, Aspergillus, Serratia, Nocardia, and Burkholderia cepacia.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A clinical laboratory receives a blood sample from a child suspected of having Chronic Granulomatous Disease. The technicians set up a flow cytometric assay using dihydrorhodamine 123. Which of the following findings is diagnostic of CGD in this clinical test?
a: Abnormal DHR test showing a lack of fluorescence
Absence of CD18 surface receptor expression
Detection of giant inclusion granules within cells
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Chronic Granulomatous Disease is evaluated and diagnosed via an abnormal dihydrorhodamine (DHR) flow cytometry test, which demonstrates a lack of rhodamine fluorescence due to absent reactive oxygen species.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A patient's neutrophils are evaluated using a Nitroblue Tetrazolium (NBT) dye reduction assay. Following chemical stimulation, the patient's cells fail to undergo a color change and remain clear/yellow. This negative NBT test result confirms a diagnosis of which of the following conditions?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: In the Nitroblue Tetrazolium (NBT) slide test, neutrophils from patients with Chronic Granulomatous Disease yield a negative NBT test because they lack the superoxide necessary to reduce the yellow dye into blue formazan.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 22-year-old male is evaluated for a suspected inherited neutrophil disorder. Laboratory investigations reveal that his leukocytes are completely unable to synthesize hypochlorous acid (HOCl) from hydrogen peroxide. Which of the following primary immunodeficiencies is characterized by this specific biochemical defect?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Myeloperoxidase (MPO) deficiency is an inherited disorder characterized by an inability of neutrophils to produce hypochlorous acid (HOCl) due to a lack of functional MPO enzymes.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: An investigator analyzes neutrophils isolated from an individual with confirmed Myeloperoxidase deficiency. During incubation with bacteria, the cells demonstrate normal activation of the membrane-bound NADPH oxidase complex and consume normal amounts of oxygen. Which of the following physiological features characterizes this specific condition?
a: Completely absent generation of superoxide anions
Failure of phagosomes to fuse with lysosomes
acute_inflammatory_response.l: Patients with Myeloperoxidase (MPO) deficiency have a completely normal respiratory burst because their upstream NADPH oxidase enzyme functions perfectly.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A 26-year-old male with an inherited phagocytic cell defect presents with persistent, recurrent oral and systemic infections caused by Candida albicans. Aside from these fungal complications, he remains relatively healthy. This clinical pattern is highly characteristic of which of the following disorders?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: While frequently asymptomatic, patients with Myeloperoxidase (MPO) deficiency who do develop infections characteristically show an increased susceptibility to disseminated Candida infections.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: A patient suspected of having a primary leukocyte killing defect undergoes diagnostic testing. Activated neutrophils are mixed with Nitroblue Tetrazolium (NBT) dye. The cells successfully generate superoxide, and the slide turns a dense, dark blue color. This positive NBT test result is characteristic of which of the following conditions?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The Nitroblue Tetrazolium (NBT) test is positive (turns blue) in Myeloperoxidase (MPO) deficiency because superoxide production remains intact, allowing dye reduction.

acute_inflammatory_response.q: Neutrophils from a 19-year-old asymptomatic female are subjected to flow cytometric analysis using dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR). Following chemical stimulation, a normal shift in green fluorescence is detected, indicating intact generation of upstream reactive oxygen species. Which of the following conditions is consistent with this normal DHR test result?
acute_inflammatory_response.l: The dihydrorhodamine (DHR) flow cytometry test is normal in individuals with Myeloperoxidase (MPO) deficiency because they generate normal amounts of peroxide substrate.
